Therefore, there remained for them no way to claim the Lordship of their partners alongside Allah, the Glorified, except to deny His uniqueness, the Exalted, in creation and origination, and to establish [creative power] for their gods after creation and origination—which they do not do. This is what necessitated His, the Exalted, mentioning this possibility to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without addressing them with it or commanding him to address them.

It is as if He, the Exalted, by saying: **أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed similar to them?" — is saying to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The argument has been completed against these people regarding the monotheism of Lordship from the perspective of His exclusivity, the Exalted, in creation and origination. Thus, nothing remains for them except to claim the partnership of their partners in creation and origination. So, are they saying that their partners created a creation like His creation, such that the creations seemed similar to them, and they consequently claimed their Lordship generally alongside Allah?"

Then He commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to deliver to them that which cuts off the root of this possibility, saying: **قُلِ اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** — "Say, ‘Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount’." The beginning of the sentence is a claim whose proof is its end; i.e., He, the Exalted, is One in His creativity, having no partner therein. How could He have a partner therein when He possesses a Oneness (وحدة) that subdues (تقهر) every number and multiplicity? We have already presented some discussion on the meaning of Him, the Exalted, being "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار) in the commentary on His saying: **أَأَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** — "Are discrete lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" (12:39). It was explained there that the combination of these two attributes yields the attribute of Uniqueness (الأحدية).

From what we have mentioned, the reason for the change in style in His saying: **أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed similar to them?" — and the turning away from the previous style of address has become clear. Reflect on this, and know that most commentators were confused regarding the arguments established by the Quranic verses to prove His Lordship, the Exalted, and His Oneness therein, and to negate partners from Him; they mixed them with the arguments established to prove the existence of the Maker (الصانع). So take note of this.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people" (13:7). He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**أنا المنذر وعلي الهادي**" — "I am the warner and Ali is the guide..." [the hadith].

**I say:** This meaning was narrated by al-Kulayni in *al-Kafi*, al-Saduq in *al-Ma'ani*, al-Saffar in *al-Basa'ir*, al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi in their respective *tafsirs*, and others through many different chains of narration.

The meaning of his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "I am the warner and Ali is the guide" is: "I am the instance (مصداق) of the warner—and warning (الإنذار) is guidance (هداية) accompanied by a call (دعوة)—and Ali is the instance of the guide without a call, and he is the Imam." It does not mean that the "warner" is exclusively the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the "guide" is exclusively Ali (peace be upon him), for that would completely contradict the apparent meaning of the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by Ibn Jarir, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu'aym in *al-Ma'rifah*, al-Daylami, Ibn Asakir, and Ibn al-Najjar, who said: When **إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people" — was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) placed his hand on his chest and said: "**أنا المنذر**" — "I am the warner," and he gestured with his hand to the shoulder of Ali and said: "**أنت الهادي ـ يا علي بك يهتدي المهتدون من بعدي**" — "You are the guide—O Ali, through you the guided shall be guided after me."

**I say:** It was also narrated by al-Tha'labi in *al-Kashf* from Ata ibn al-Sa'ib, from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In *Mustadrak al-Hakim*, with its chain of narration from Ibrahim ibn al-Hakam ibn Zahir, from his father, from al-Hakam ibn Jarir, from Abu Buraydah al-Aslami, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called for water for purification while Ali ibn Abi Talib was with him. After the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had purified himself, he took Ali's hand and pressed it to his [the Prophet's] chest, then said: "**إنما أنت منذر**" — "You are only a warner," meaning himself. Then he moved it to Ali's chest and said: "**ولكل قوم هاد**" — "and there is a guide for every people." Then he said to him: "**أنت منار الأنام وغاية الهدى وأمير القراء ـ أشهد على ذلك أنك كذلك**" — "You are the lighthouse of mankind, the ultimate goal of guidance, and the leader of the reciters—I bear witness to that, that you are indeed so."

**I say:** It was also narrated by Ibn Shahrashub from al-Hakim in *Shawahid al-Tanzil*, and al-Marzubani in *Ma Nazala min al-Quran fi Amir al-Mu'minin*.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, narrated by Abdullah ibn Ahmad in *Zawa'id al-Musnad*, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat*, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people." He said: "**رسول الله المنذر وأنا الهادي**" — "The Messenger of Allah is the warner and I am the guide." In another wording: "**والهادي رجل من بني هاشم**" — "And the guide is a man from Banu Hashim," meaning himself.

**I say:** There are many other narrations from Sunni sources regarding this meaning.

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people." He said: "**كل إمام هاد لكل قوم في زمانهم**" — "Every Imam is a guide for every people in their time."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Fudayl, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "and there is a guide for every people." He said: "**كل إمام هاد للقرن الذي هو فيهم**" — "Every Imam is a guide for the generation in which he resides."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): **إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people." He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**أنا المنذر وعلي الهادي**" — "I am the warner and Ali is the guide." [Then he said:] "**يا با محمد هل من هاد اليوم؟**" — "O Abu Muhammad, is there a guide today?" I said: "May I be your ransom, there has never ceased to be a guide from among you after a guide, until it reached you." He said: "**رحمك الله يا با محمد لو كانت إذا نزلت آية على رجل ـ ثم مات ذلك الرجل ماتت الآية مات الكتاب ـ ولكنه يجري فيمن بقي كما جرى فيما مضى**" — "May Allah have mercy on you, O Abu Muhammad. If it were that when a verse was revealed concerning a man, and then that man died, the verse died and the Book died—but rather, it flows (يجري) for those who remain just as it flowed for those who passed."

**I say:** The narration testifies to what we previously stated—that the inclusion of Ali (peace be upon him) in the verse is by way of "flow" (الجري), and it similarly flows for the rest of the Imams. This "flow" is what is intended by the reports stating that it was revealed concerning Ali (peace be upon him).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى وَما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ** — "Allah knows what every female carries, and what the wombs stint" (13:8). He said: "**ما لم يكن حملا**" — "That which is not a fetus." [And regarding] **وَما تَزْدادُ** — "and what they add" — he said: "**الذكر والأنثى جميعا**" — "The male and female together."

**I say:** His saying "the male and female together" means what exceeds one [fetus] from a single [conception], as evidenced by the following narration.

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn Muslim and others, from both of them (peace be upon them), they said: **ما تَحْمِلُ** — "what [every female] carries" — refers to every female or male; **وَما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ** — "and what the wombs stint" — he said: "**ما لم يكن حملا**" — "that which is not a fetus"; **وَما تَزْدادُ** — "and what they add" — from female or male.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hariz, from one whom he mentioned, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them) regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى وَما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ وَما تَزْدادُ** — "Allah knows what every female carries, and what the wombs stint and what they add." He said: "**الغيض كل حمل دون تسعة أشهر**" — "The stinting (الغيض) is every pregnancy less than nine months," and **وَما تَزْدادُ** — "and what they add" — is everything that increases beyond nine months. For whenever a woman sees pure blood during her pregnancy from menstruation, she adds [to the term] the number of days she saw blood during her pregnancy.

**I say:** This is another meaning, and it has been reported from some of the early commentators.

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of narration from Tha'labah ibn Maymun, from some of our companions, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ** — "the Knower of the Unseen and the witnessed" (13:9). He said: "**الغيب ما لم يكن والشهادة ما قد كان**" — "The Unseen (الغيب) is what has not yet been, and the witnessed (الشهادة) is what has already been."

**I say:** What is meant by "what has not yet been" is not the non-existent (المعدوم) that is nothing, but rather the matter that is in potentiality (بالقوة) as long as it has not entered the realm of actuality (الفعلية). What he (peace be upon him) mentioned is one of the instances (المصاديق), which is apparent.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, narrated by Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in *al-Kabir*, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Dala'il*, through the path of Ata ibn Yasar, from Ibn Abbas: That Arbad ibn Qays and Amir ibn al-Tufayl came to Madinah to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). They reached him while he was sitting and sat before him. Amir said: "What will you give me if I submit?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**لك ما للمسلمين وعليك ما عليهم**" — "You shall have what the Muslims have and be bound by what they are bound by." He said: "Will you give me the authority after you if I submit?" He said: "**ليس لك ولا لقومك ولكن لك أعنة الخيل**" — "It is not for you nor for your people, but you shall have the reins of the cavalry." He said: "Then give me the desert (الوبر) and you take the settled land (المدر)." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**لا**" — "No." When he turned to leave, he said: "I will surely fill it against you with horses and men." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**يمنعك الله**" — "Allah will prevent you."

When Arbad and Amir left, Amir said: "O Arbad, I will distract Muhammad from you with talk, so strike him with the sword. For if you kill Muhammad, the people will do no more than be satisfied with the blood money (الدية) and will dislike war, so we will give them the blood money." Arbad said: "I will do so." They returned, and Amir said: "O Muhammad, stand with me so I may speak to you." He stood with him, and they went aside to a wall. Amir stood talking to him while Arbad drew his sword. When he placed his hand on his sword, it froze upon the hilt, and he could not draw it. Arbad delayed in striking, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) turned and saw Arbad and what he was doing, and he walked away from them. Amir said to Arbad: "What is wrong with you, you coward?" He said: "I placed my hand on the hilt of the sword and it froze."

When Amir and Arbad left the presence of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and reached Harrah Raqam, they stopped. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh and Usayd ibn Hudayr came out to them and said: "Be gone, O enemies of Allah! May Allah curse you both," and they rebuked them. Amir said: "Who is this, O Sa'd?" Sa'd said: "This is Usayd ibn Hudayr al-Kata'ib." He said: "By Allah, Hudayr was a friend of mine."

When he was at al-Raqam, Allah sent a thunderbolt upon Arbad and killed him. Amir went on until he was at al-Khurayb, where Allah sent a plague-sore (قرحة) upon him, and death overtook him there. Then Allah revealed: **اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى** — "Allah knows what every female carries..." until His saying: **لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ** — "He has guardian angels (المعقبات) before him..." (13:8-11). He said: The guardian angels (المعقبات) are by the command of Allah, protecting Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Then He mentioned Arbad and what killed him, saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ** — "It is He who shows you the lightning..." until His saying: **وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ** — "...and He is terrible in retributive might" (13:12-13).

**I say:** Something similar in meaning was narrated from al-Tabari and Abu al-Shaykh from Ibn Zayd, and at its end: Labid said concerning his brother Arbad, weeping for him:

*I fear the fates for Arbad, yet I do not*
*Dread the stars of the sky nor the lions.*
*The thunder and thunderbolts have bereaved me of the knight*
*On the day of the fierce, valiant battle.*

What the narration mentions regarding the revelation of these verses in this story does not align with the context of the Surah's verses, which clearly indicate it is Meccan. Furthermore, it does not suit the context of the verses themselves based on the meaning we have already established.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur* as well, narrated by Ibn al-Mundhir and Abu al-Shaykh from Ali: **لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ** — "He has guardian angels before him and behind him, who guard him by Allah’s command" (13:11). He said: "**ليس من عبد إلا ومعه ملائكة ـ يحفظونه من أن يقع عليه حائط أو يتردى في بئر ـ أو يأكله سبع أو غرق أو حرق ـ فإذا جاء القدر خلوا بينه وبين القدر**" — "There is no servant except that there are angels with him, guarding him from a wall falling on him, or falling into a well, or being eaten by a predator, or drowning, or burning. But when the decree (القدر) comes, they leave him to the decree."

**I say:** Something similar in meaning was also narrated from Abu Dawud in *al-Qadar*, Ibn Abi al-Dunya, and Ibn Asakir from him. Something similar was also narrated from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Fudayl ibn Uthman, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): He said: He narrated to us this verse: **لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ** — "He has guardian angels before him and behind him." He said: "**من المقدمات المؤخرات ـ المعقبات الباقيات الصالحات**" — "From the preceding and succeeding ones—the 'successive ones' (المعقبات) are the 'enduring righteous deeds' (الباقيات الصالحات)."

**I say:** Its apparent meaning is that the enduring righteous deeds (الباقيات الصالحات) are among the instances of the "successive ones" (المعقبات) mentioned in the verse, which protect their performer from an evil decree. They only protect through the angels appointed over them, so its meaning returns to what we presented in the explanation of the verse. It is also possible that the "preceding and succeeding ones" are the enduring righteous deeds themselves, and its return to what we presented is apparent.

In the same source, from Abu Amr al-Mada'ini, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: My father used to say: "**إن الله قضى قضاء حتما ـ لا ينعم على عبد بنعمة ـ فسلبها إياه قبل أن يحدث العبد ما ـ يستوجب بذلك الذنب سلب تلك النعمة ـ وذلك قول الله : إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ ـ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ**" — "Indeed, Allah has decreed a definitive decree: He does not bestow a blessing upon a servant and then strip it from him before the servant commits that which necessitates the stripping of that blessing due to that sin. That is the saying of Allah: 'Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls' (13:11)."

In the same source, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ ـ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَإِذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً ـ فَلا مَرَدَّ لَهُ** — "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls. And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it" (13:11). He said: "**فصار الأمر إلى الله تعالى**" — "Thus the matter rests with Allah the Exalted."

**I say:** This is an allusion to the meaning of the verse we previously presented.

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of narration from Abdullah ibn al-Fadl, from his father, who said: I heard Abu Khalid al-Kabuli say: I heard Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) say: "**الذنوب التي تغير النعم ـ البغي على الناس والزوال عن العادة في الخير ـ واصطناع المعروف وكفران النعم وترك الشكر ، قال الله عز وجل : إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ ـ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ**" — "The sins that change blessings are: transgressing against people, departing from the habit of doing good and performing kindness, ingratitude for blessings, and abandoning thanks. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls' (13:11)."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from al-Hasan ibn Faddal, from al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً** — "It is He who shows you the lightning, sealing fear and hope" (13:12). He said: "**خوفا للمسافر وطمعا للمقيم**" — "Fear for the traveler and hope for the resident."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Nu'mani, from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, from Ali (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ** — "and He is terrible in retributive might" (13:13). He said: "**يريد المكر**" — "He means 'the stratagem' (المكر)."

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with its chain of narration from Anas ibn Malik: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent a man to one of the tyrants (فراعنة) of the Arabs to call him to Allah, Mighty and Majestic. He said to the messenger: "Tell me about this one to whom you call me—is He of silver, or of gold, or of iron?" The messenger returned to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and informed him of his words. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**ارجع إليه فادعه**" — "Return to him and call him." He said: "O Prophet of Allah, he was more obstinate than that." He said: "**ارجع إليه**" — "Return to him." He returned and the man said the same as before. While he was speaking to him, a cloud thundered a single clap and cast a thunderbolt upon his head, taking away the crown of his head. Then Allah, Great is His Praise, revealed: **وَيُرْسِلُ الصَّواعِقَ فَيُصِيبُ بِها مَنْ يَشاءُ ـ وَهُمْ يُجادِلُونَ فِي اللهِ وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ** — "He unleashes the thunderbolts and strikes with them whomever He wishes, while they dispute concerning Allah, and He is terrible in retributive might" (13:13).

**I say:** The discussion regarding its end is like the discussion regarding the end of what preceded in the story of Amir and Arbad. This report adds that His saying: **وَيُرْسِلُ الصَّواعِقَ** — "He unleashes the thunderbolts..." etc., is part of a verse, and there is no reason for the verses to be fragmented in their revelation.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, he said: In a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ** — "and there is a guide for every people" (13:7): "As for those who prostrate from the inhabitants of the heavens willingly, they are the angels—they prostrate to Allah willingly. As for those who prostrate from the inhabitants of the earth from among those born into Islam, they prostrate to Him willingly. As for those who prostrate to Him unwillingly, they are those who were forced into Islam. And as for those who do not prostrate, their shadows prostrate to Him in the morning and evening."

**I say:** The apparent meaning of the narration contradicts the context of the noble verse. For the verse is intended to explain the universality of His subdual (قهره), the Exalted, through His greatness and loftiness, of whoever is in the heavens and the earth—themselves and their shadows—and it informs of their prostration to Him, the Exalted, with the reality of prostration (حقيقة السجدة). The apparent meaning of the narration is that prostration means "falling down" (الخرور) and placing the forehead, or what resembles prostration, which is universally present either in them or in their shadows. If they prostrate literally, whether willingly or unwillingly, then so be it; otherwise, the falling of their shadows upon the ground resembles prostration. This is a meaning that does not reflect the majesty of Allah, the Great, the All-Exalted.

Furthermore, it does not agree with the universality apparent in His saying: **وَظِلالُهُمْ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ** — "and their shadows [as well], morning and evening" — and even clearer than that is the universality in His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى ما خَلَقَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا ظِلالُهُ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَالشَّمائِلِ سُجَّداً لِلَّهِ وَهُمْ داخِرُونَ وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ دابَّةٍ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ** — "Have they not regarded the things Allah has created? Their shadows shift to the right and the left, prostrating to Allah in utter humility. To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of animals and angels, and they are not arrogant" (16:48-49).
